item.title: Konataci targets strong performance at Glasgow Commonwealth Games
item.description: Konataci , who is from Burerua in Tailevu, departed last Thursday ahead of the Games in Glasgow, Scotland. He is Fiji’s only para-athlete competing in the men’s discus throw at the Games.
item.content:encoded:
Konataci , who is from Burerua in Tailevu, departed last Thursday ahead of the Games in Glasgow, Scotland.

He is Fiji’s only para-athlete competing in the men’s discus throw at the Games.

Konataci will compete in the Ambulant Men's Discus Throw on Wednesday, 29th July in Glasgow.

He says preparations have been going well, although improving his throwing distance has been challenging at times.

Konataci says this will be his first Commonwealth Games and his first opportunity to represent Fiji on a major international sporting stage.

He says he is proud of the achievement and plans to focus on what he has been practising during training.

Konataci says if everything goes well, his goal is to compete for a gold medal.

The former Tailevu North College student says he competed in throwing events during secondary school and has continued to work hard despite challenges.

He is urging people in Fiji to support and pray for him as he prepares to represent the country.

The 23rd Commonwealth Games will be held in Glasgow, Scotland, from next Friday to 2nd August.

item.title: Fiji could qualify for FIFA World Cup within the next 10 years - Fiji FA
item.description: While speaking to fijivillage Sports, Fiji Football Association Chief Executive Officer Mohammed Yusuf says with 211 FIFA member associations competing for a place at the World Cup, qualification is never easy and the Fiji FA is taking a long-term approach to developing the sport from the grassroots to the elite level. He says that since 2017, Fiji has qualified for FIFA youth tournaments, giving
item.content:encoded:
While speaking to fijivillage Sports, Fiji Football Association Chief Executive Officer Mohammed Yusuf says with 211 FIFA member associations competing for a place at the World Cup, qualification is never easy and the Fiji FA is taking a long-term approach to developing the sport from the grassroots to the elite level.

He says that since 2017, Fiji has qualified for FIFA youth tournaments, giving young players valuable international experience.

Yusuf says this year, Fiji has once again earned qualification at the youth level, demonstrating that the country's football development pathway is producing results.

The CEO also stressed that one of the key initiatives is the expansion of youth football through the McDonald's League, which provides structured competition for Under-14, Under-16 and Under-19 players and the scho ols football, including the Under-18 competitions, and employers' are also helping to identify and develop talen ted players from an early age.

Yusuf stresses that off the field, Fiji Football continues to invest in infrastructure like the development work at Lomaloma Stadium and the establishment of a High Performance Centre, including a FIFA-supported artificial pitch expected to begin in stages from August to October, are designed to provide players with world-class training facilities.

Yusuf says as Fiji continues to compete in Oceania Football Confederation (OFC) competitions, hopes remain high that one day the senior national team will reach the FIFA World Cup.

item.title: World Cup winners to receive about FJ$112 million in record prize money
item.description: The winners of the 2026 FIFA World Cup will receive about FJ$112 million, the biggest prize ever awarded in football. FIFA is expected to generate about FJ$29 billion in revenue from the tournament, while the total prize pool for the 48 participating teams will be around FJ$1.94 billion.
item.content:encoded:
The winners of the 2026 FIFA World Cup will receive about FJ$112 million, the biggest prize ever awarded in football.

FIFA is expected to generate about FJ$29 billion in revenue from the tournament, while the total prize pool for the 48 participating teams will be around FJ$1.94 billion.

Every nation that qualifies for the World Cup will receive at least FJ$27.9 million, including a participation fee and funding to help cover tournament preparations.

FIFA says most of its revenue comes from broadcasting rights, ticket sales, sponsorships and licensing , with the expanded 104-match tournament expected to generate significantly more income than previous editions.

The world football governing body says it plans to reinvest at least FJ$26.1 billion into the development of football worldwide . Source: Aljazeera

item.title: Lack of focus from the start cost us the game - Seruvakula
FIFA says no evidence ball hit overhead cable before England goal
item.description: FIFA says there is no evidence the ball touched the overhead Skycam cable before England's controversial equaliser against Norway in their World Cup quarterfinal. The governing body says data from the Connected Ball Technology showed no contact with the cable, confirming the officials made the correct decision to allow play to continue.
item.content:encoded:
FIFA says there is no evidence the ball touched the overhead Skycam cable before England's controversial equaliser against Norway in their World Cup quarterfinal.

The governing body says data from the Connected Ball Technology showed no contact with the cable, confirming the officials made the correct decision to allow play to continue.

Norway players and coach Ståle Solbakken disputed the decision, claiming the ball struck the overhead cable during the build-up to Jude Bellingham's goal.

Despite the controversy, England defeated Norway 2-1 after extra time.

England will now face Argentina in the World Cup semifinals at 7am on Thursday, while France will take on Spain at 7am on Wednesday.

item.title: Quirke replaces injured Mitchell in England squad
item.description: Mitchell suffered the injury during England's 73-8 win over the FIJI Water Flying Fijians in Liverpool last Saturday. The 24-year-old Quirke recently signed with Newcastle Red Bulls after leaving Sale Sharks.
item.content:encoded:
Mitchell suffered the injury during England's 73-8 win over the FIJI Water Flying Fijians in Liverpool last Saturday.

The 24-year-old Quirke recently signed with Newcastle Red Bulls after leaving Sale Sharks.

He has made two appearances for England and became the country's youngest scrum-half in more than 90 years when he made his debut in 2021.

Quirke also played for an England XV against

a France XV in a Nations Championship warm-up match last month.

Leicester's Jack van Poortvliet and Bath's Ben Spencer are the other scrum-half options for coach Steve Borthwick ahead of England's final Test of the summer against Argentina.

Meanwhile, in other games this weekend, the Flying Fijians will take on Scotland at Murrayfield, Edingburgh at 1.10am on Sunday, South Africa will play Wales at 3.40am, and Argentina will take on England at 7.10am.

On Saturday, New Zealand will go against Ireland at 7.10pm, Japan will play France at 8.40pm, and Australia will host Italy in Perth at 10.10pm.

item.title: McGregor injures leg in UFC return, referee stops fight

item.title: England edge Norway in extra time to reach FIFA World Cup semi finals
item.description: England have edged Norway 2-1 in extra time this morning in Miami to book their place in the FIFA World Cup semi finals.Jude Belli

item.title: Kane and Haaland set for showdown as England face Norway
item.description: England will take on Norway for a place in the FIFA World Cup semi finals.The winner will face either Argentina or Switzerland in
